label: in-voice
text: Most LLM tooling treats Claude as a wrapper around an API. A function gets a model call wrapped around it, the model generates text, the text gets parsed back into the function's vocabulary. The model is the verb; the tool is plumbing.

label: in-voice
text: This server inverts the relationship. Claude is the reasoner over taste primitives. The tools encode constraints, not capabilities. Three patterns, deliberately: retrieval, critique, mutation.

label: in-voice
text: No tool reaches for the model. The model reaches for the tools. The case study below is itself the artifact: this page was drafted, voice-checked, and scaffolded by Claude through the same five tools it documents.

label: in-voice
text: Document processing pipelines look simple on a slide. Extract. Validate. Mask. Queue exceptions. Ship. In practice they're a black-box workflow with the model in the middle, doing everything from OCR to business rules to deciding what's PII. Hard to debug, hard to cost, hard to swap a stage out.

label: in-voice
text: This pipeline pulls those concerns apart. Five MCP tools, one per stage. The model is the orchestrator, not the pipeline. Each tool owns its piece of the contract.

label: in-voice
text: Five tools, one orchestrator. Each stage is a separate MCP tool, swappable in isolation. The orchestrator is the model. Three of the five tools are mutations; one is critique; one is aggregation. The pipeline is composition.

label: in-voice
text: Five primitives, three shapes. Pattern, name, role. Each tool ships in a single file under src/tools/. Schemas are Zod, validated on every call.

label: in-voice
text: Constraints, encoded. The voice rules from CLAUDE.md, expressed as lexical detectors. Read aloud: hedge words, filler transitions, marketing warmth. The tool flags every match with a verbatim excerpt and a one-line suggestion.

label: in-voice
text: Five MCP tools that expose the creative practice as primitives — vault search, palette suggestion, voice critique, project scaffolding, vault capture. The server returns candidates and rules; the model does the picking and the rewriting. Proves: tool-use schema design, MCP server engineering, and taste encoded as constraints rather than agents.

label: in-voice
text: Five MCP tools, one pipeline. Extract structured JSON, mask PII, validate against schema and business rules, route exceptions to a human queue, report token cost. The model composes; each tool owns one contract. Proves: document processing, structured outputs, PII masking, HITL routing, and token-cost optimization.

label: in-voice
text: Real-time rigid-body physics with synthesized audio on collision, a scoring system, combo multiplier, and CHAOS MODE. Built to argue that the demo can be the product. Proves: motion engineering + sound design + game feel, drafted in code from sketch to ship.

label: in-voice
text: palette_suggest doesn't rank — it returns candidates and lets the model reason. The candidates already carry their author's notes. Ranking would discard the notes; reasoning over them is the value.

label: in-voice
text: voice_check refuses to rewrite. The split between what's wrong (tool) and what to write instead (model) is intentional. Rewriting is taste; flagging is rules. They don't belong in the same surface.

label: in-voice
text: I design and build. Both. Same hand, same deadline, same screen. I built systems before I designed them. CS degree. Master's in computer graphics technology. Fifteen years writing code that runs in production.

label: in-voice
text: I am not interested in surface-level futurism or empty tech style. I am interested in interaction craft, operational beauty, and the interface side of AI products. 6% is how much the right change shifts the entire product. Design Engineer is the discipline that makes it possible.

label: off-voice

label: off-voice
text: In terms of design philosophy, I tend to believe that simplicity is sort of the ultimate sophistication. That said, when it comes to product engineering, perhaps the most important thing is shipping. Therefore I focus on the work that maybe matters most.

label: off-voice
text: To be honest, the journey to where I am today has been incredible. I've been passionate about technology since I was young, and that passion has driven me to dive deep into the craft and emerge with insights that I'm excited to share.

label: off-voice

label: generic
text: I'm a senior product designer with over a decade of experience building digital products for technology companies. I work across the full design process, from research and strategy through to interface design and prototyping.

label: generic
text: Design engineer focused on web interfaces, motion, and design systems. I work with teams to ship interactive products and improve the quality of their design implementation.

label: generic
text: A web-based tool for visualizing time-series data. Includes filters, custom dashboards, and exportable reports. Built with React, D3.js, and a Node.js backend.

label: generic
text: An e-commerce checkout redesign that reduced cart abandonment. Worked closely with the engineering team to ship a new flow informed by usability testing and analytics review.

label: generic
text: A design system for a fintech company. Documented components, established design tokens, and built a Figma library used across multiple product teams.

label: generic
text: This project explored how AI could improve the user experience of a complex enterprise tool. The team worked through several iterations of the interface, gathering feedback from users at each stage and refining the design based on what we learned.

label: generic
text: The goal of this redesign was to make the product easier to use for new customers. We mapped the existing onboarding flow, identified friction points, and built a new version that addressed the main issues uncovered in the research.

label: generic
text: A common challenge in B2B software is balancing power and simplicity. This case study describes how the design team approached that tension on a recent project, and the trade-offs that came up along the way.

label: generic
text: When I joined the team, the design process was disconnected from the engineering work. Over the following months, we worked to bring the two functions closer together and improve the velocity of shipping new features.

label: generic
text: Design systems are a way for product teams to ship consistently and quickly. This project documents the system we built, the components it includes, and how it was adopted across the organization over the course of a year.

label: generic
text: The approach. The team used a research-driven process to inform every design decision. Insights from interviews and usability tests shaped the direction at each stage of the project.

label: generic
text: What we built. The final product is a web application with a redesigned interface, a new onboarding flow, and improved performance across the most-used screens.

label: generic
text: How it ships. The system is deployed via a continuous integration pipeline, with automated tests running on every commit. The team can release new versions on demand.

label: generic
text: After several rounds of iteration, the team settled on a final design that addressed the original goals. The implementation took approximately three months and involved coordination across design, engineering, and product management.

label: generic
text: The metrics improved after launch. We saw measurable gains in the key indicators the team had been tracking, including conversion rate and time-to-completion for the primary user journey.
